content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Mike                                          Date : Sept. 15, 1977

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Ted

Subject: Doing business in the jurisdiction

The following are the results of some preliminary research I did on
the corporate registration requirements in California. Corporations
Code¹/ Section 2105 provides that a foreign corporation may not do
intra state business in California without first obtaining a certi-
ficate of qualification. Section 171 provides that a foreign corpora-
tion does not include any domestic corporation or for Chapter 21
any corporation chartered under the laws of the United States.
Section 167 defines a domestic corporation as any corporation formed
under California law. Section 2105 also does not apply to any
proprietorship or partnership.

Section 191 defines transacting intra state business to mean entering
into repeated and successive transactions of its business in this
state other than interstate or foreign commerce. The reference to
interstate or foreign commerce means engaging exclusively in inter-
state or foreign commerce in the constitutional sense. Section 191
provides that a parent is not deemed to be doing business in the
state merely because its subsidiary is doing business in the state.
The following is a partial list of activities which are specifically
exempted from the notion of intra state business:

     1.    Litigation

     2.    Conducting meetings

     3.    Bank accounts

     4.    Maintaining offices for registration or depositors securities

     5.    Effecting sales through independent contractors

     6.    Soliciting sales which must be accepted outside the state

     7.    Creating evidences of duties etc.


_____________________________
1/ All references are to the Corporations Code unless
otherwise noted.

Mike
Sept. 15, 1977
Page Two


     8.  Conducting isolated transactions completed within 180
          days

     9.  Numerous activities related to lending institutions

As mentioned previously, proprietorships and partnerships are not
required to register with the state. They may, however, be required
to register with local communities pursuant to local business license
ordinances. I do not know whether a corporation registered with
the state must also register with local communities pursuant to
these business license ordinances.

Sometime before the opinion is written on the question of doing
business in the jurisdiction, I would like to discuss the problems
of notice particularly with respect to doing business in local
jurisdictions. I think it will be relatively easy to determine
when a corporation is doing business in California, particularly if
we use registration or chartering as the determining factor. However,
I am not sure that reference to local business license ordinances
is a satisfactory way of determining whether an entity is doing
business within a local jurisdiction. I suspect these local ordinances
differ widely in their requirements. I still am not certain that
minimum contacts analysis really provides enough notice to people
when it comes to determining whether an entity is doing business in
a local jurisdiction.


TP:plh

sections.question_synthetic: The document discusses California corporate registration requirements for foreign corporations doing intrastate business, including exemptions and the applicability of local business license ordinances.

Corporations Code¹/ Section 2105 provides that a foreign corporation may not do intra state business in California without first obtaining a certi- ficate of qualification. Section 171 provides that a foreign corpora- tion does not include any domestic corporation or for Chapter 21 any corporation chartered under the laws of the United States. Section 167 defines a domestic corporation as any corporation formed under California law. Section 2105 also does not apply to any proprietorship or partnership. Section 191 defines transacting intra state business to mean entering into repeated and successive transactions of its business in this state other than interstate or foreign commerce. The reference to interstate or foreign commerce means engaging exclusively in inter- state or foreign commerce in the constitutional sense. Section 191 provides that a parent is not deemed to be doing business in the state merely because its subsidiary is doing business in the state. The following is a partial list of activities which are specifically exempted from the notion of intra state business: 1. Litigation 2. Conducting meetings 3. Bank accounts 4. Maintaining offices for registration or depositors securities 5. Effecting sales through independent contractors 6. Soliciting sales which must be accepted outside the state 7. Creating evidences of duties etc. _____________________________ 1/ All references are to the Corporations Code unless otherwise noted. Mike Sept. 15, 1977 Page Two 8. Conducting isolated transactions completed within 180 days 9. Numerous activities related to lending institutions As mentioned previously, proprietorships and partnerships are not required to register with the state. They may, however, be required to register with local communities pursuant to local business license ordinances. I do not know whether a corporation registered with the state must also register with local communities pursuant to these business license ordinances. Sometime before the opinion is written on the question of doing business in the jurisdiction, I would like to discuss the problems of notice particularly with respect to doing business in local jurisdictions. I think it will be relatively easy to determine when a corporation is doing business in California, particularly if we use registration or chartering as the determining factor. However, I am not sure that reference to local business license ordinances is a satisfactory way of determining whether an entity is doing business within a local jurisdiction. I suspect these local ordinances differ widely in their requirements. I still am not certain that minimum contacts analysis really provides enough notice to people when it comes to determining whether an entity is doing business in a local jurisdiction. TP:plh
embedding.summary: This is an internal FPPC memorandum from Ted to Mike dated September 15, 1977, presenting preliminary research on California Corporations Code requirements for foreign corporations conducting intrastate business, including registration requirements, exemptions, and questions about local business license ordinances.
